nativeRange.habitat: Clear to slightly turbid hill streams, rivers, and forest streams with sandy or gravel substrate, often with leaf litter and submerged roots; also found in lowland rivers and reservoirs

decorPreferences: open swimming space for active shoaling
leaf litter for natural biotope effect
robust plants (Java fern, Anubias, Vallisneria) along margins
dim areas or floating plants to diffuse light

diet.notes: Accepts a wide variety of foods. Staple of high-quality flake, pellets, or granules supplemented with frozen or live foods such as bloodworms, brine shrimp, and daphnia. Vegetable matter (blanched spinach, zucchini, spirulina wafers) is also readily taken. Varied diet helps maintain health and colour. Feed 2–3 times daily in small portions.

goodWith: larger robust barbs (tinfoil barb, denison barb, rosy barb)
medium to large characins (larger tetras, silver dollars)
avoidWith: long-finned fish (angelfish, bettas, fancy guppies — fin-nipping risk)
small fish that may be intimidated or nipped

imageCandidates.notes: Check FishBase species page for Barbodes lateristriga for current representative image; URL is illustrative — verify at https://www.fishbase.se/summary/Barbodes-lateristriga.html

summary: The Spanner Barb is a robust, active Southeast Asian cyprinid reaching up to 18 cm, named for the T-shaped or wrench-like (spanner-like) dark body markings. It requires a spacious tank of 200 L or more, a tight-fitting lid, and must be kept in groups of six or more. A known fin-nipper — best housed with robust, similarly sized tankmates.
careNotes:
The Spanner Barb (Barbodes lateristriga) is one of the larger barb species available in the hobby, with adults regularly reaching 15–18 cm. A tank of at least 200 L with a minimum 120 cm footprint is required to accommodate a proper shoal and the species’ energetic swimming behaviour. This is a powerful, active fish and an excellent jumper — a tight-fitting lid is essential.

Keep in groups of at least six; smaller groups lead to increased intraspecific and interspecific aggression, and individuals kept alone or in pairs can become highly fin-nippy toward tankmates. In a large group, aggression is spread more evenly and the fish settle into a natural hierarchy. Water should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 5–15), reflecting the hill-stream and forest-stream habitats of Peninsular Malaysia, Thailand, and the Indonesian archipelago. Good filtration and regular water changes are important; the species is tolerant of a range of conditions but does best in clean, well-oxygenated water with gentle to moderate current.

The species was historically placed in Puntius and then Systomus before Pethiyagoda et al. (2012) reclassified it into the resurrected genus Barbodes. Older literature and trade labels may still use Puntius lateristriga or Systomus lateristriga. The common names “Spanner Barb” and “T-Barb” both refer to the distinctive body pattern: a vertical bar near the head and a horizontal bar along the flank form a shape resembling a spanner (wrench) or the letter T. This pattern is most vivid in juveniles and can fade somewhat in adults, especially in large males. Breeding coloration in males includes intensified reddish-orange on the fins.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is possible but not commonly achieved by beginners, placing this species in the moderate difficulty bracket for breeding. The species is an egg-scatterer. Condition adults on live and frozen foods. A separate, dimly lit breeding tank of at least 80 L with a fine-leaved plant mat or spawning mop and a mesh or pebble substrate (to protect eggs from being eaten) is recommended. Slightly acidic, soft water (pH 6.5, gH 4–6, temperature 26–28°C) and a small powerhead to simulate gentle current may trigger spawning. Remove adults immediately after spawning. Eggs hatch in 24–48 hours; fry are free-swimming within a further 2–3 days and accept infusoria and micro-foods initially, transitioning to baby brine shrimp as they grow.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Barbodes lateristriga
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 18 cm), temperature range (22–28°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness, native range (Thailand, Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, Borneo), habitat (hill streams, lowland rivers), diet, behaviour (active shoaler, fin-nipper), and breeding notes. Confirmed current valid name Barbodes lateristriga (Pethiyagoda et al. 2012 reclassification from Systomus/Puntius).

additional.name: FishBase — Barbodes lateristriga
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max reported 20 cm TL in wild; aquarium max typically 18 cm), native range (Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia, Singapore, Cambodia), taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), temperature and pH parameters. Confirmed synonyms including Puntius lateristriga and Systomus lateristriga.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Barb Species Guide
additional.notes: General barb care reference; confirmed fin-nipping behaviour, need for groups of 6+, and intermediate care level designation for larger barb species. Supplemented tank size and compatibility guidance.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Spanner Barb species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed common names (Spanner Barb, T-Barb), adult size to 18 cm, care level intermediate, need for large tank (200 L+), shoaling requirement, and fin-nipping temperament. Cross-checked water parameters and SE Asian native range.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Cyprinidae of Southeast Asia
additional.notes: Supplementary reference for Pethiyagoda et al. 2012 Barbodes reclassification context and historical placement in Puntius/Systomus. General Cyprinidae Southeast Asia coverage.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer. Requires separate breeding tank with spawning mop or fine plants and egg-protective substrate. Adults must be removed post-spawning. Soft, slightly acidic warm water (pH 6.5, 26–28°C) with gentle current recommended. Fry accept infusoria initially then baby brine shrimp.
